title:
Module 4
SFW Video Creation
subtitle: Learn Motion Control with Kling and How to Generate Video Prompts

blocks.content: Video content gets 3-4x the reach of static images on every major platform. Instagram Reels, TikTok, and even Threads with attached video all prioritize motion content in their algorithms. If you are only posting images, you are leaving the majority of your growth potential on the table.

Video makes your AI persona feel real and alive. A 5-second clip of your model blinking, smiling, and turning her head does more for believability than 50 static photos. Moving content stops the scroll in a way that images cannot. This module covers how to create video content using Kling AI and how to turn raw clips into polished, platform-ready content.

items: Instagram Reels reach 3-10x more non-followers than carousels or feed posts
TikTok's entire algorithm is built around video discovery
Video content converts followers to subscribers faster than any other format
Even short 3-5 second clips massively boost engagement rates
Pages that post Reels daily grow 4-5x faster than image-only pages
Video is the highest-value content type for subscriber platforms
Turn a single static image into a short video clip (3-10 seconds)
Add natural motion: hair movement, blinking, smiling, head turns
Control camera movement: slow zoom, pan, tracking
Generate lip sync — your model appears to speak with synced lips
Produce videos in multiple aspect ratios (9:16, 1:1, 16:9)
Extend existing clips to create longer sequences

left.title: IMAGE PLACEHOLDER
left.content: Screenshot of Kling AI dashboard/interface showing the image-to-video workflow. Label key areas: upload source image, prompt input, generation settings, output preview.

right.content: Kling Modes — Which to Use When
Kling offers different generation modes. Understanding when to use each one saves credits and produces better results.

items: Standard Mode — Lower quality, faster generation, cheaper. Use for testing prompts and ideas before committing credits
Professional Mode — Higher quality, slower, costs more credits. Use for final content that will be posted publicly
Always test your prompt in Standard Mode first, then regenerate in Professional Mode once you are happy with the motion

right.content: Kling Credit Economics
Understanding the cost structure helps you budget and avoid wasting credits on bad generations.

items: Free tier: Limited daily generations (good for learning, not for production)
Paid plans: $10-$30/month depending on usage tier
Standard Mode uses fewer credits per generation
Professional Mode uses 2-3x more credits than Standard
Budget approximately $15-$25/month for a consistent posting schedule
Tip: batch your video generation sessions to maximize efficiency

right.content: When to Use Video

items: Instagram Reels — Primary use case, highest reach multiplier
TikTok posts — Strong discovery platform for video content
Instagram Stories — 'Casual' feeling clips increase authenticity
Content that requires motion to feel real (smiling, hair movement, walking)

right.title: Cost-Saving Rule
right.content: Never burn Professional Mode credits on an untested prompt. Always test in Standard Mode first. One test generation costs a fraction of a final render. This habit alone will save you 30-40% of your monthly credit spend.

sections.header: Lesson 2: Writing Video Prompts for Kling

blocks.content: Kling video generation is prompt-driven, just like image generation. But video prompts require you to think about motion, timing, and camera behavior — not just static appearance. The key difference: a good video prompt describes what happens, not just what the scene looks like. Less is more with video — simpler motion produces more realistic results.

items: Start with the source image (your AI model image from Module 2/3)
Describe the motion: what moves and how (keep it simple)
Describe camera behavior: static, slow zoom, gentle pan
Describe the mood: calm, energetic, playful, intimate
Keep it to one motion at a time — less motion equals more realistic output

left.content: Understanding this scale is critical. The further right you go, the more likely the output will look glitchy or unnatural.

items: Simple (best results): Blink, soft smile, hair movement in wind, slow breathing
Medium (good results): Head turn, slow camera zoom, slight body lean, looking up/down
Complex (risky): Full body movement, walking, camera panning, multiple motions at once
Too Complex (will fail): Dancing, running, fast camera movement, multiple people interacting

left.title: IMAGE PLACEHOLDER
left.content: Motion complexity scale visual: horizontal gradient from green (Simple) through yellow (Medium) to red (Too Complex). Show example motions at each level: blink → head turn → walking → dancing.

right.content: 10 Proven Video Prompts

1. 'Slow zoom in on face, she looks at camera and gives a soft smile, slight head tilt to the right, natural daylight'

2. 'Hair gently blowing in the wind, slow blink, looking directly at camera, golden hour lighting'

3. 'Slight head turn from left to right, soft smile appears, warm indoor lighting, static camera'

4. 'Looking down at phone, then slowly looks up at camera with eye contact, natural lighting'

5. 'Slow zoom in, she tucks hair behind ear, slight smile, bedroom setting'

6. 'Sitting on couch, slow breathing motion, looks up from book and makes eye contact, warm lighting'

7. 'Mirror reflection, she adjusts top slightly, gentle head tilt, bathroom lighting'

8. 'Close-up face, slow blink, wind gently moves hair, direct eye contact, outdoor'

9. 'Leans chin on hand, slow smile develops, camera slowly zooms in, cafe setting'

10. 'Standing, slight weight shift side to side, looks at camera over shoulder, natural light'

Bad prompts:
'Girl dancing and spinning around' — Too much motion, will distort
'Camera flies around her while she walks' — Impossible for AI
'She picks up coffee, drinks it, puts it down' — Multiple sequential actions fail

All bad prompts try to do too much at once. One motion. One camera move. That is it.

right.title: IMAGE PLACEHOLDER
right.content: Side by side: output from a simple prompt (clean, natural) vs output from an overloaded prompt (distorted, glitchy). Show the quality difference clearly.

blocks.title: Golden Rule for Video
blocks.content: Source image quality matters more than generation settings. A perfect source image with a simple prompt will always outperform a mediocre source image with a complex prompt. Start with your best images from Module 3.

Lip sync is one of the most powerful features in Kling. It makes your AI model appear to speak with synced lip movements. A 5-second clip of your model 'talking' directly to camera is incredibly convincing and dramatically outperforms standard motion clips for engagement. This is what makes your persona feel real.

items: Upload your source image (clear front-facing shot, mouth visible)
Provide audio input — either record a voice clip or use text-to-speech
Kling syncs the lip movements to match the audio
Output: your model appears to be speaking the words naturally
Works best with clear, front-facing portraits where the mouth is fully visible
Use a clear, well-lit front-facing image — the face must be prominent
Audio should be clear with minimal background noise
Keep clips short: 3-7 seconds produces the most natural results
Simple phrases work better than long monologues
Text-to-speech voice should match your persona's aesthetic (young, warm, casual)
Review output carefully — lip sync can sometimes drift or look unnatural on certain sounds
'Hey, come talk to me' — Simple CTA for Stories
'Good morning' / 'Goodnight' — Daily check-in clips
Trending audio lip sync — Matches popular Reels format
Short personality statements — 'I swear I was going to study today'
Response clips — Appear to reply to comments or questions

right.content: Video Extension
Raw Kling output is typically 3-5 seconds. For a 15-30 second Reel, you need to create multiple clips and stitch them together, or use Kling's video extension feature.

items: Video Extend lets you continue a generated clip for additional seconds
Each extension adds 3-5 more seconds of motion
The AI tries to continue the natural motion from where the clip ended
Works best when the original clip ends in a natural position (not mid-motion)
Quality can degrade with multiple extensions — limit to 1-2 extensions per clip
For longer content, stitch separate clips together in an editor (next lesson)

right.content: Face Preservation Tips
The biggest challenge with AI video is maintaining your character's face consistency. Small errors compound over frames — by the end of a long clip, the face can drift.

items: Use your best, clearest reference image as the source — quality in = quality out
Subtle motion preserves faces better than dramatic movement
Conservative movement settings reduce face drift
Short clips (3-5 seconds) have better face consistency than long ones
Always compare the first and last frame — if the face drifted, regenerate
Professional Mode produces more consistent faces than Standard Mode

right.title: IMAGE PLACEHOLDER
right.content: Lip sync workflow: Source Image + Audio Input → Kling Lip Sync → Output video showing model appearing to speak. Annotate the steps clearly.

blocks.content: Raw Kling output is only half the job. A 3-5 second silent clip is not a finished Reel. This lesson covers how to edit clips together, add trending audio, and create polished content that performs on Instagram and TikTok. The editing step is what transforms raw AI video into content that competes with real influencers.

left.content: You do not need expensive software. These free tools handle everything you need for social media video.

items: CapCut (Desktop & Mobile) — Best for beginners, designed for social media, free
DaVinci Resolve (Desktop) — More powerful, free version is fully capable
InShot (Mobile) — Quick mobile editing for Stories and simple clips
CapCut is recommended for most operators — it has Reels-specific templates and trending audio built in

left.content: A finished Reel is typically 10-30 seconds. Since Kling produces 3-5 second clips, you need to combine multiple clips into one seamless video.

items: Generate 3-5 separate clips of your model with different motions
Import all clips into CapCut or DaVinci Resolve
Arrange them in a natural sequence (e.g., look up → smile → hair flip → look away)
Add smooth transitions between clips (crossfade or cut on motion)
Trim any awkward frames from the beginning or end of each clip
Target total length: 10-15 seconds for Reels, 15-30 seconds for TikTok

left.title: IMAGE PLACEHOLDER
left.content: Timeline view in CapCut showing 3-4 Kling clips arranged sequentially with transitions between them, audio track below, and text overlay. Annotate: clips, transitions, audio, text.

right.content: Adding Audio — The Growth Multiplier
Trending audio is what makes Reels go viral. The right sound can 10x your reach. Never post a Reel without audio.

items: Use Instagram's built-in audio library to find trending sounds
Check the Reels tab daily — trending audio changes every 1-2 weeks
Match the mood of the audio to your clip (romantic, chill, energetic)
CapCut has a trending audio section specifically for Reels/TikTok
Lip sync audio: use your generated lip sync as the audio track
Never use copyrighted music directly — use platform-provided audio only

right.content: The 15-Second Reel Formula
This formula consistently produces high-performing Reels. Follow it until you find what works specifically for your audience.

items: Second 0-1: Hook — Face, eye contact, immediate attention grab
Second 1-5: First motion — Smile, head turn, or hair movement
Second 5-8: Cut to new angle or setting — Different clip, same model
Second 8-12: Audio peak — Sync a key moment to the audio's strongest beat
Second 12-15: End — Soft look at camera or look away. Leave them wanting more

right.title: IMAGE PLACEHOLDER
right.content: The 15-second Reel formula as a horizontal timeline: Hook (0-1s) → Motion (1-5s) → Cut (5-8s) → Audio Peak (8-12s) → End (12-15s). Color-coded segments with descriptions.

blocks.title: Pro Tip
blocks.content: Start simple. A 5-second clip of your AI model looking at the camera with a subtle smile, a slow zoom-in, and a trending audio track will outperform a complex, glitchy 15-second video every time. Master the basics before trying advanced editing. Quality always beats complexity.

The final step is exporting your edited video in the correct format for each platform. Wrong resolution, wrong aspect ratio, or wrong duration means your content underperforms or gets cropped awkwardly. This lesson covers the exact export settings for every platform.

items: Instagram Reels: 1080x1920 (9:16 vertical), MP4, 15-30 seconds ideal
Instagram Stories: 1080x1920 (9:16 vertical), MP4, under 15 seconds
Instagram Feed: 1080x1080 (1:1 square) or 1080x1350 (4:5), MP4
TikTok: 1080x1920 (9:16 vertical), MP4, 15-60 seconds
Twitter/X: 1280x720 minimum, MP4, under 2 minutes
Always export as MP4 — it is universally supported
Face matches your base model consistently throughout the clip
No weird hand or finger movements visible
Motion looks natural, not robotic or jerky
No visual glitches, warping, or frame jumps
Audio (if added) syncs properly — no lag or desync
First frame shows face clearly (this is the thumbnail/hook)
Text overlays are readable and not cut off by UI elements
Grain has been added to the source images before video generation

right.content: Platform Posting Strategy

items: Instagram Reels — Add trending audio, 3-5 hashtags, and a hook caption. Post during peak hours (check Insights)
Instagram Stories — Use raw-feeling clips for authenticity. No heavy editing needed. Post 3-5 per day
TikTok — Similar to Reels but keep it more casual. Native-feeling content outperforms polished content
Twitter/X — Short clips with captions. Video tweets get 10x more engagement than text-only

right.content: Content Batching for Video
Video generation is slower than image generation. Batch your video work to maximize efficiency.

items: Generate all Kling clips in one session (5-10 clips at a time)
Edit all clips in one editing session
Export multiple versions: Reels format, Story format, TikTok format
Schedule posts across the week — never post all videos on the same day
Keep a video backlog of 7-10 finished clips ready to post
One dedicated video session per week is better than daily scrambling

right.title: IMAGE PLACEHOLDER
right.content: Export settings reference card: Platform → Resolution → Aspect Ratio → Ideal Duration → Format. Cover all platforms in a clean table format.

blocks.title: Video Priority Rule
blocks.content: Video content should be your #1 priority for Instagram and TikTok growth. A page with daily Reels will grow 4-5x faster than a page posting only images. If you can only do one thing per day, make it a Reel. Everything else supports the video-first strategy.

summary_points: Video content gets 3-4x more reach than images — it should be your #1 growth priority
Kling AI turns static images into realistic video clips with motion, expressions, and camera movement
Always test prompts in Standard Mode before committing Professional Mode credits — saves 30-40% of costs
Keep video prompts simple — one motion at a time produces the most realistic results
Lip sync is one of the most powerful features — a 5-second talking clip dramatically boosts believability
Edit clips together using CapCut (free) — combine 3-5 short clips with transitions and trending audio
The 15-second Reel formula: Hook → Motion → Cut → Audio Peak → End
Export in 9:16 at 1080x1920 for Reels/TikTok, always MP4, add trending audio before posting
Batch video generation and editing sessions weekly — keep a backlog of 7-10 finished clips ready to post
